expanded_description: Points, lines, and planes are the fundamental building blocks of geometry. These undefined terms form the foundation upon which all geometric definitions, postulates, and theorems are constructed. Understanding their properties and relationships is essential for studying shapes, angles, proofs, and spatial reasoning.

core_concept.principle: Geometry begins with three undefined terms—point, line, and plane—that are accepted without formal definition but understood through their properties and postulates. These basic elements describe location, direction, and flat surfaces in space.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a point as a precise location marker, like the tip of a sharpened pencil touching paper. A line is like a laser beam shooting infinitely in both directions with no curve. A plane is like a perfectly flat tabletop that extends forever without edges. These concepts cannot be defined using simpler terms, so we describe them through their behaviors and the rules (postulates) that govern them.

image.alt: Visual showing a dot labeled Point A, a straight line with arrows at both ends labeled Line l passing through points B and C, and a shaded parallelogram labeled Plane M. Caption: 'The Three Undefined Terms: Point, Line, Plane.'
image.caption: The Undefined Terms of Geometry: Point (location), Line (straight path), and Plane (flat surface)—the foundation of all geometric reasoning.

sections.section_title: The Undefined Terms: Point, Line, and Plane
sections.introduction: In geometry, we begin with three terms that are not formally defined—point, line, and plane. Instead of definitions, we describe them using characteristics and accepted postulates. All other geometric figures are built from these basic elements.

key_concept.definition: A point indicates a location and has no dimension (no length, width, or thickness). A line is a straight, one-dimensional path that extends infinitely in two opposite directions and has no thickness. A plane is a flat, two-dimensional surface that extends infinitely in all directions and has no thickness.
key_concept.context: These undefined terms avoid circular definitions. By starting with accepted primitive concepts, geometry builds a logical system where every other term (segment, ray, angle, polygon) can be precisely defined using points, lines, and planes.

classifications.value: A location in space with zero dimensions—no length, width, or thickness.
characteristics: Represented by a dot and named with a single capital letter (e.g., Point A, Point P)
Has position but absolutely no size
Is the simplest geometric element—cannot be divided
All geometric figures are ultimately sets of points
classifications.behavior: Points mark exact positions. They define locations, endpoints of segments, vertices of angles, and intersections of lines. A point has no parts.

examples.explanation:
Point P is drawn as a small dot labeled P.
We say 'Point P' and understand the dot is only a model—the actual geometric point has no size.

When two distinct lines cross, they meet at exactly one point.
When a line passes through a plane, they intersect at exactly one point.

classifications.value: A straight, continuous arrangement of infinitely many points extending without end in two opposite directions.
characteristics: Has one dimension: length only (no width, no thickness)
Named using any two distinct points on it (e.g., Line AB, written with double arrow: AB↔)
Can also be named with a single lowercase script letter (e.g., Line ℓ)
Arrows on both ends of a diagram indicate infinite extension
classifications.behavior: Lines provide the concept of straightness and infinite direction. Through any two distinct points there is exactly one line. Between any two points on a line, there are infinitely many other points.

examples.process: Naming a line by two points
examples.explanation:
Line AB (AB↔) passes through points A and B and extends infinitely in both directions.
The double arrow symbol ↔ indicates infinite extension.

examples.process: Naming a line with a single letter
examples.explanation: Line ℓ (lowercase script letter) provides a concise way to refer to a line without listing points.

classifications.value: A flat surface that extends infinitely in all directions, containing infinitely many points and lines.
characteristics: Has two dimensions: length and width (no thickness)
Named using a capital script letter (e.g., Plane M) or any three noncollinear points on it (e.g., Plane ABC)
Extends without boundary in all directions along its surface
Represented in diagrams as a shaded parallelogram (showing only a portion)
classifications.behavior: Planes provide the concept of flatness. Through any three noncollinear points there is exactly one plane. If two points of a line lie in a plane, the entire line lies in that plane.

examples.process: Naming a plane by three points
examples.explanation:
Plane ABC is determined by noncollinear points A, B, and C.
Any three points not on the same line define a unique flat surface.

examples.process: Naming a plane with a letter
examples.explanation: Plane M (capital script letter) is a single-name identifier for the infinite flat surface.

image.alt: Three labeled panels: (1) A dot labeled A with note '0 dimensions', (2) A line with arrows at both ends passing through points B and C with note '1 dimension', (3) A shaded parallelogram representing a plane containing multiple points and a line with note '2 dimensions'.
image.caption: The Three Undefined Terms: Point (0D), Line (1D), and Plane (2D)—each building upon the previous.

sections.introduction: Points are classified by their relationships to lines and planes. Two essential terms—collinear and coplanar—describe whether points lie on the same line or in the same plane. These relationships are critical for understanding geometric figures and spatial reasoning.

key_concept.definition: Collinear points are points that lie on the same straight line. Coplanar points are points that lie in the same plane. These relationships determine whether points can form lines, triangles, or three-dimensional figures.
key_concept.context: Understanding collinearity and coplanarity allows us to determine when points define a unique line or plane, when three points form a triangle, and whether geometric objects exist in two dimensions or require three dimensions.

classifications.value: Points that lie on the same straight line.
characteristics: Any two distinct points are always collinear (they determine exactly one line)
Three or more points may or may not be collinear
If three points are collinear, exactly one of them lies between the other two
Collinear points share the property that slopes between any two are equal
classifications.behavior: Collinear points belong to a single line. Noncollinear points cannot all fit on one line—at least one point lies off the line determined by the others.

examples.explanation:
Points A, B, and C lie on Line ℓ. They are collinear.
Through any two of them, the line is identical—Line AB is the same as Line BC.

Points D, E, and F are noncollinear if no single line passes through all three.
Three noncollinear points determine a triangle and exactly one unique plane.

classifications.value: Points that lie in the same plane.
characteristics: Any three points are always coplanar (they determine at least one plane)
Four or more points may or may not be coplanar
If two points lie in a plane, the entire line through them lies in that plane
All points on a line are coplanar with any single point not on that line
classifications.behavior: Coplanar points share a single flat surface. Noncoplanar points cannot all fit in one plane—they define three-dimensional space (like the four vertices of a triangular pyramid).

examples.explanation:
Points P, Q, R, and S all lie in Plane M. They are coplanar.
Any line connecting two of them also lies entirely in Plane M.

Four points are noncoplanar if no single plane contains all four.
Example: The four vertices of a tetrahedron (triangular pyramid) are noncoplanar.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Collinear and Coplanar Relationships
worked_examples.problem:
Points A, B, and C lie on line ℓ. Point D is not on line ℓ.
1) Are A, B, C collinear?
2) Are A, B, D collinear?
3) Are A, B, C, D coplanar?
4) Could these four points be noncoplanar?

step_by_step.explanation: Yes. By definition, points that lie on the same line are collinear. All three lie on line ℓ.
No. Points A and B determine line ℓ, but D is not on line ℓ. Since D is not on the unique line through A and B, the three points cannot be collinear.

step_by_step.step: 3) Coplanarity of A, B, C, D
step_by_step.explanation: Yes. Points A and B (on line ℓ) together with point D (not on ℓ) are three noncollinear points. They determine exactly one plane. Since C lies on line ℓ, and two points of ℓ (A and B) are in that plane, the entire line ℓ—including point C—lies in that same plane.

No. The four points must be coplanar because: A, B, D (noncollinear) fix a unique plane, and C is on line AB which lies entirely in that plane by the postulate. Noncoplanar points would require a point not in the plane defined by any three.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Yes, A, B, C are collinear
2) No, A, B, D are noncollinear
3) Yes, all four points are coplanar
4) No, these four points cannot be noncoplanar
worked_examples.concept_applied: Collinear points lie on one line. Coplanar points lie in one plane. A line and a point not on it determine a plane that contains the entire line.

sections.introduction: Postulates (also called axioms) are statements accepted as true without proof. They describe the fundamental properties of points, lines, and planes, and serve as the starting point for all geometric reasoning and proofs.

key_concept.definition: Postulates are the foundational rules that govern how points, lines, and planes exist and interact. They establish existence, uniqueness, and intersection properties that every theorem in geometry ultimately depends upon.
key_concept.context: These postulates form the axiomatic foundation of Euclidean geometry. Every theorem about triangles, circles, and spatial relationships traces back to these basic accepted truths.

process_flow.description: Through any two distinct points there is exactly one line.
process_flow.mechanism: Given two different points, one and only one straight line can be drawn passing through both. This guarantees uniqueness—if two lines share two distinct points, they must be the same line.
process_flow.result: Any two distinct points fix a unique line. This is why we can name a line using any two points on it.
applications: Given points A and B, exactly one line passes through both
If Line m and Line n both contain points P and Q, then Line m = Line n
Used to prove that two lines are identical

process_flow.process: Three Noncollinear Points Determine a Plane
process_flow.description: Through any three noncollinear points there is exactly one plane.
process_flow.mechanism: If three points do not all lie on the same line, they fix exactly one flat surface. No other plane can contain all three. This is why we name planes using three noncollinear points.
process_flow.result: Any three noncollinear points fix a unique plane. A triangle (three noncollinear vertices) always lies in exactly one plane.
applications: Points A, B, C (noncollinear) determine Plane ABC uniquely
A triangle always lies in exactly one plane
Used to define faces of three-dimensional polyhedra

process_flow.description: If two points of a line lie in a plane, then the entire line lies in that plane.
process_flow.mechanism: If a line has two of its points on a flat surface, every other point on that line must also lie on that surface. The line is a subset of the plane—it cannot pop out of the plane.
process_flow.result: A line is entirely contained within any plane that contains two of its points. This connects lines to planes.
applications: If A and B are in Plane M, then the entire line AB↔ lies in Plane M
Used to prove additional points are coplanar
Explains why a straightedge drawn on flat paper stays on the paper

process_flow.description: If two distinct lines intersect, they intersect in exactly one point.
process_flow.mechanism: Two different straight lines can cross at most once. If they shared two or more points, by Postulate 1 they would be the same line. Therefore, intersecting lines meet at a single, unique point.
process_flow.result: The intersection of two distinct intersecting lines is exactly one point—the point of intersection. This point is the vertex of the angles formed.
applications: Line AB↔ and Line CD↔ intersect at exactly one point
Defines the vertex of an angle (where two rays meet)
Used to study vertical angles and angle relationships

process_flow.description: If two distinct planes intersect, they intersect in exactly one line.
process_flow.mechanism: When two flat surfaces in space cross each other, their common points form a straight line. That line of intersection contains every point that belongs to both planes.
process_flow.result: The intersection of two intersecting planes is exactly one line. This line is the edge where the two flat surfaces meet.
applications: Two walls of a room meet at a corner—their intersection is a vertical line
A wall and the floor meet at a horizontal line
Used to define edges of three-dimensional solids like cubes and pyramids

image.alt: Five illustrated panels: (1) Two points with exactly one line through them, (2) Three noncollinear points defining a shaded plane, (3) A line with two points on a plane—entire line shown on the plane, (4) Two lines crossing at exactly one point labeled 'intersection', (5) Two shaded planes intersecting along one line labeled 'line of intersection'.
image.caption: The Five Fundamental Postulates: Rules governing how points, lines, and planes interact.

sections.section_title: Defined Terms: Segments, Rays, and Opposite Rays
sections.introduction: From the undefined term 'line,' we define important subsets. A segment is a finite portion of a line. A ray is a half-line starting at an endpoint. Opposite rays together form an entire line. These defined terms are essential for discussing length, direction, and angles.

key_concept.definition: A line segment is part of a line consisting of two endpoints and all points between them. A ray is part of a line consisting of one endpoint and all points extending infinitely in one direction. Opposite rays are two rays that share an endpoint and extend in exactly opposite directions, together forming a line.
key_concept.context: Segments allow measurement of finite distances. Rays provide the concept of direction from a starting point. Opposite rays and their common endpoint define the structure of a line. Two rays sharing an endpoint form an angle—a concept explored in the next topic.

classifications.value: A part of a line bounded by two distinct endpoints.
characteristics: Has a definite, measurable length (the distance between endpoints)
Named by its two endpoints with a bar over the letters (e.g., Segment AB, written AB—)
Contains the endpoints and every point between them
Order of endpoints does not matter: AB— = BA—
classifications.behavior: Segments are finite, measurable portions of lines. They form the sides of polygons and edges of polyhedra. The length AB (without bar) is a numerical distance.

examples.explanation:
Segment AB (written AB with a bar over the letters) has endpoints A and B.
It includes points A, B, and all points between them on the line through A and B.

AB (without bar) denotes the length—the distance between A and B.
If A and B are 5 cm apart, then AB = 5 cm.

classifications.value: A part of a line with exactly one endpoint that extends infinitely in one direction.
characteristics: Has an endpoint (starting point) but no definite length
Named starting with the endpoint, then another point on the ray (e.g., Ray AB where A is the endpoint)
Symbol: AB→ (arrow pointing right over the letters)
Order matters: Ray AB ≠ Ray BA (different endpoints, different directions)
classifications.behavior: Rays represent directional paths originating from a point. Two rays that share a common endpoint form an angle. A ray is essentially half of a line (one direction from the endpoint).

examples.explanation:
Ray AB (AB→) starts at endpoint A, passes through B, and continues infinitely beyond B.
Ray BA (BA→) starts at endpoint B, passes through A, and continues infinitely beyond A.
These are completely different rays.

Ray PQ and Ray PR with the same endpoint P form ∠QPR.
Point P is the vertex of the angle; Rays PQ and PR are the sides.

classifications.value: Two rays that share the same endpoint and extend in exactly opposite directions along the same line.
characteristics: Together they form a complete line
Share a common endpoint
Are collinear (lie on the same line)
Point in exactly opposite directions
If B is between A and C, then Ray BA and Ray BC are opposite rays
classifications.behavior: Opposite rays are always collinear. Their common endpoint is the dividing point between the two directions. Every point on the line belongs to one of the two opposite rays (or is the shared endpoint).

examples.explanation:
On a line, point B lies between A and C.
Ray BA (starts at B, goes toward A) and Ray BC (starts at B, goes toward C) are opposite rays.
Together: Ray BA ∪ Ray BC = entire line AC↔.

Ray PQ and Ray PR forming an angle at P are NOT opposite rays.
They are not collinear—they point in different directions that are not opposite.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Segments, Rays, and Opposite Rays
worked_examples.problem:
Points A, B, and C lie on a line in that order (B is between A and C).
1) Name the segment with endpoints A and C.
2) Name the ray starting at A passing through C.
3) Name the ray starting at B going toward A.
4) Identify any pair of opposite rays with endpoint B.
5) Does Ray AB equal Ray AC?

step_by_step.explanation: Segment AC (AC—) consists of endpoints A and C with all points between them—which includes point B. It is finite with length AC.
Ray AC (AC→) has endpoint A, passes through B, continues through C, and extends infinitely beyond C. The endpoint is A.
Ray BA (BA→) has endpoint B, passes through A, and extends infinitely beyond A. It points in the direction opposite to C.
Ray BA and Ray BC share endpoint B, lie on the same line, and point in opposite directions. They are opposite rays. Together they form the entire line through A, B, and C.
Yes, Ray AB and Ray AC are the same ray. Both have endpoint A and extend in the same direction (through B and C). Since B and C lie on the same ray from A, the ray is named correctly either way.

image.alt: A horizontal line showing points A, B, C in order from left to right. Segment AC highlighted between A and C. Ray AC shown with arrow extending right from A through C. Ray BA shown with arrow extending left from B through A. Opposite rays BA and BC shown with arrows in opposite directions from B.
image.caption: Segments, Rays, and Opposite Rays: Visual comparison of segment AC, Ray AC, Ray BA, and opposite rays BA and BC.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Segment AC (AC—)
2) Ray AC (AC→)
3) Ray BA (BA→)
4) Rays BA and BC are opposite rays
5) Yes, Ray AB and Ray AC are the same ray
worked_examples.concept_applied: Segments have two endpoints and finite length. Rays have one endpoint and infinite extension in one direction. Opposite rays share an endpoint and form a line. A ray can be named using the endpoint and any other point on it.

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes with Points, Lines, and Planes
sections.introduction: Several misconceptions frequently arise when first learning about the foundations of geometry. Recognizing these errors early prevents confusion in more advanced topics involving angles, triangles, and proofs.

classifications.value: Believing that a point is a tiny circle with measurable diameter or area.
characteristics: Error: Treating a point as a small filled-in circle with physical dimensions
Correct: A point has zero dimensions—no length, width, or area. It is a location only.
classifications.behavior: The dot drawn on paper is merely a representation or model. The actual geometric point is an exact location with no size whatsoever.

examples.explanation: The ink dot representing Point A has physical size on paper, but the mathematical Point A it represents has no size at all—it is pure location.

classifications.note: Solution: Think of a point as an exact address or coordinate—a location, not an object. The dot is just a placeholder.

classifications.type: Believing Lines and Planes Are Finite
classifications.value: Thinking a line ends at the diagram's edges or that a plane stops at the parallelogram border.
characteristics: Error: A line drawn between A and B 'stops' at those points. A plane is only the shaded parallelogram shown.
Correct: Lines extend infinitely in both directions. Planes extend infinitely in all directions along the surface.
classifications.behavior: Arrowheads on line diagrams indicate infinite extension. The parallelogram representing a plane is only a portion of the infinite surface.

examples.explanation: A line drawn with arrows at both ends continues forever past any points shown. Points A and B are just two of infinitely many points on that infinite line. Only segments have definite endpoints.

classifications.note: Solution: Look for arrowheads to confirm infinite extension. If there are no arrowheads and endpoints are marked, it may be a segment, not a line.

classifications.value: Claiming that any three points determine a unique plane, without checking collinearity.
characteristics: Error: 'Three points always determine exactly one plane.'
Correct: Three NONCOLLINEAR points determine exactly one plane. Three collinear points lie in infinitely many planes.
classifications.behavior: If three points lie on the same straight line, infinitely many planes can contain that line (imagine planes rotating around the line like a hinge). The 'noncollinear' condition is essential.

examples.explanation:
Points A, B, and C all on the same line. There are infinitely many planes containing that line (think of a door swinging—the hinge line is the line through A, B, C; the door panel represents different planes).
Only three noncollinear points fix a single, unique plane.

classifications.note: Solution: Always verify: are the three points noncollinear? If they lie on the same line, the plane is not unique.

classifications.value: Assuming ray names are interchangeable because both contain the same two points.
characteristics: Error: Ray AB and Ray BA are the same thing.
Correct: Ray AB has endpoint A. Ray BA has endpoint B. They have different starting points and point in opposite directions.
classifications.behavior: For rays, the first letter is always the endpoint—the starting location. Reversing the order reverses the direction and changes the endpoint.

examples.explanation:
Ray PQ has endpoint P and goes toward (and past) Q.
Ray QP has endpoint Q and goes toward (and past) P.
These are different rays unless P = Q. They point in opposite directions along the same line.

classifications.note: Solution: For rays, first letter = endpoint (where it starts). Order is critically important. For segments, order does not matter (AB— = BA—).

worked_examples.title: Identifying and Correcting Foundation Errors
worked_examples.problem:
A student writes the following statements. Identify and correct each error:
1) 'A line segment extends infinitely in both directions.'
2) 'Any three points determine exactly one plane.'
3) 'Ray AB and Ray BA are the same ray because they both contain points A and B.'

step_by_step.explanation:
Error: Confusing a segment with a line. A segment is a finite portion with two endpoints.
Correct: A line segment has two endpoints and a definite, measurable length. It does NOT extend infinitely. A line extends infinitely.

Error: Omitting the critical condition 'noncollinear.'
Correct: Through any three NONCOLLINEAR points there is exactly one plane. Three collinear points lie in infinitely many planes.

Error: Assuming ray names are interchangeable like segment names.
Correct: Ray AB has endpoint A and extends toward B. Ray BA has endpoint B and extends toward A. They are different rays (opposite directions). For segments, AB— = BA—; for rays, AB→ ≠ BA→.

worked_examples.final_answer:
Corrected statements:
1) A line segment has two endpoints and finite length (not infinite).
2) Three NONCOLLINEAR points determine exactly one plane.
3) Ray AB and Ray BA are different rays with different endpoints and opposite directions.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Segments vs. lines, the noncollinear condition for the plane postulate, and ray naming convention (first letter is always the endpoint).

sections.section_title: Real-World Applications of Points, Lines, and Planes
sections.introduction: The abstract concepts of points, lines, and planes model countless real-world situations. Recognizing these connections makes geometry meaningful and demonstrates its practical power in design, navigation, construction, and technology.

categories.description: Points, lines, and planes are fundamental to representing locations, paths, and regions on maps and in coordinate systems.

examples.explanation: A GPS location is essentially a geometric point on Earth's surface—specified by latitude and longitude. The point has no size, just a precise numerical location.

The shortest path between two cities on Earth follows the great circle through those two points. This 'line' on the spherical surface is determined by the two endpoints, analogous to the line postulate in plane geometry.
Property lines are segments with endpoints marked by survey monuments. The straight-line boundaries between adjacent properties are geometric segments.

categories.description: Building design and construction rely directly on geometric principles of points, lines, and planes.

examples.process: Walls, floors, and ceilings as planes
examples.explanation: A wall is a portion of a plane. The corner where two walls meet is the line of intersection of two planes. The corner where floor and two walls meet is the intersection point of three planes.
Construction blueprints use reference points and datum lines to locate every feature. Builders snap chalk lines to transfer geometric lines from plans to the actual construction surface.

categories.description: Visual perspective and 3D modeling are applications of projective geometry built on points, lines, and planes.

examples.explanation: Vanishing points and horizon lines in perspective drawing are geometric points and lines. Parallel lines in 3D space appear to converge at a vanishing point on the horizon line—the intersection of the ground plane and the image plane.

3D models define objects using vertices (points), edges (line segments), and faces (polygonal regions of planes). Rendering engines calculate intersections of light rays (lines) with object surfaces (planes) to create realistic images.

sections.section_title: Practice with Points, Lines, and Planes
sections.introduction: Building confidence with the foundations of geometry requires practice identifying elements, applying postulates, and reasoning about spatial relationships. Work through these problems to test your understanding.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Practice Set
worked_examples.problem:
1) How many lines can pass through a single point?
2) How many planes can contain three collinear points?
3) Points A, B, C, D are noncoplanar. What is the intersection of Plane ABC and Plane ABD?
4) If two distinct lines intersect, how many planes contain both lines?
5) True or False: Ray PQ and Ray QP are opposite rays.
6) Points X, Y, Z are noncollinear. How many planes contain all three?

step_by_step.explanation: Infinitely many. Through a single point, you can draw lines in infinitely many directions. Only through TWO distinct points is the line unique (Postulate 1).
Infinitely many. Three collinear points lie on a single line, and infinitely many planes can contain that line (like pages of a book sharing the spine). They do NOT determine a unique plane.

Plane ABC and Plane ABD share points A and B. Since the points are noncoplanar, these are two distinct planes. By Postulate 5, their intersection is exactly one line—Line AB. (They share the line through A and B.)

Exactly one. Two intersecting lines share their intersection point. Take that point and one other point from each line—you have three noncollinear points, which determine exactly one plane (Postulate 2). Both lines lie in that plane.

False. Opposite rays must share the same endpoint. Ray PQ has endpoint P; Ray QP has endpoint Q. They have different endpoints, so they cannot be opposite rays—even though they point in opposite directions along the same line.
Exactly one. By Postulate 2, through any three noncollinear points there is exactly one plane. Points X, Y, Z are noncollinear, so they determine a unique plane.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Infinitely many
2) Infinitely many
3) Line AB
4) Exactly one plane
5) False (opposite rays must share an endpoint)
6) Exactly one plane
worked_examples.concept_applied: Postulates 1, 2, and 5; definitions of collinear, noncollinear, ray, and opposite rays. Spatial reasoning about lines and planes through points.

summary: Points, lines, and planes are the three undefined terms that form the foundation of geometry. Key points: 1) Point: A location with zero dimensions (0D), named with a capital letter. A point has position but no size. 2) Line: A straight one-dimensional path extending infinitely in two directions (1D), named by two points (AB↔) or a lowercase letter (ℓ). 3) Plane: A flat two-dimensional surface extending infinitely (2D), named by three noncollinear points (Plane ABC) or a capital script letter. 4) Collinear/Coplanar: Collinear points lie on the same line (any two are always collinear). Coplanar points lie in the same plane (any three are always coplanar). 5) Five Key Postulates: (i) Two points → exactly one line. (ii) Three noncollinear points → exactly one plane. (iii) If two points of a line lie in a plane, the entire line lies in that plane. (iv) Two intersecting lines → exactly one intersection point. (v) Two intersecting planes → exactly one intersection line. 6) Segments and Rays: A segment (AB—) has two endpoints and finite length. A ray (AB→) has one endpoint and extends infinitely in one direction. Opposite rays share an endpoint and extend in opposite directions, together forming a line. 7) Common errors: Thinking points have size, believing lines/planes are finite, forgetting the 'noncollinear' condition for planes, confusing ray naming order (endpoint first). These foundational concepts are the essential vocabulary for all geometry—angles, triangles, polygons, circles, and three-dimensional reasoning.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Undefined Terms' in center. Main branches: Point (0D, location), Line (1D, infinite in two directions), Plane (2D, infinite in all directions). Sub-branches: Postulates (two points→line, three noncollinear points→plane, line in plane, line intersection, plane intersection), Defined Terms (segment, ray, opposite rays), Relationships (collinear, coplanar).
review_questions_image.caption: Points, Lines, and Planes Review: A visual concept map connecting undefined terms, postulates, defined terms, and spatial relationships.
